findings.description: The claim that 'any lighter edge crossing C would also span two different components and would have been chosen earlier' is not sufficient to establish that e* is the minimum cut-edge of C = (S_i, V \ S_i): a lighter edge crossing C could connect S_i to some S_m ≠ S_j and would have been chosen earlier only if it is the globally smallest uncovered edge, but the argument as stated conflates 'globally smallest edge spanning two components' with 'minimum cut-edge of this specific cut', which requires the additional observation that e* is the globally smallest edge spanning any two components and hence also the smallest crossing any particular cut it crosses.
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify that e* is the globally smallest edge spanning two different components, so it is in particular the smallest edge crossing the cut C = (S_i, V \ S_i), since every edge crossing that cut also spans two different components and thus would have been considered before e*.
